coredata.dc:title: Preparation and properties of bifunctional Gd2O3/GQD composite nanoparticles
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Chengjie
Ou, Meigui
Zhou, Hao
Yang, Chunlin
coredata.dc:description: Nanocomposites of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified gadolinium oxide (Gd2O3) particles were successfully synthesized. In addition, the effects of the amount of GQDs and the particle size of Gd2O3 on the properties of nanocomposites were investigated. The structures and properties of nanoparticles were characterized by X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, photoluminescence spectrometry, and magnetic resonance imaging. The results show that the synthesized particles Gd2O3/GQD are spherical with a diameter of approximately 40 nm and have excellent fluorescence and magnetic property, and its relaxation rate (r 1 = 25.18 (mmol/L)−1/s) is higher than that of the typical commercial contrast agents Gd-DTPA (r 1 = 4.5 (mmol/L)−1/s). The fluorescence property of Gd2O3/GQD is optimized by varying the V GQDs:V Gd 2 O 3 ratio, and 1:1 ratio of V GQDs:V Gd 2 O 3 results in the best fluorescence property. The large particles of Gd2O3 improve the relaxation property of nanocomposites. Therefore, the nanocomposites Gd2O3/GQD with magnetic and fluorescence characteristics can be used in biological imaging, drug targeting, and in-vivo diagnostic analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gd2O3/GQD
Magnetic
Fluorescence
Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)
Rare earths

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of amount of GQDs on fluorescence property of Gd2O3/GQD
Effect of solvent on relaxation property of Gd2O3/GQD

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Education of Guizhou Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of encapsulation process of Gd2O3/GQD.
TEM images (a, b), HRTEM image (c) and SAED pattern (d) of Gd2O3/GQD.
(a) XPS spectra of total spectra; (b) C 1s spectra in GQDs and Gd2O3/GQD; (c) Gd 4d spectrum in Gd2O3/GQD; (d) FT-IR spectra of Gd2O3, GQDs and Gd2O3/GQD.
TEM (inset: HRTEM image) (a) and AFM (b) images of GQDs.

SEM images of Gd2O3/GQD1 (a), Gd2O3/GQD2 (b) and Gd2O3/GQD3 (c); Emission spectra of Gd2O3/GQD1 (d), Gd2O3/GQD2 (e) and Gd2O3/GQD3 (f); (g) fluorescence property of GQDs and Gd2O3/GQD prepared with different amounts of GQDs.
CIE chromaticity coordinates of Gd2O3/GQD1, Gd2O3/GQD2, and Gd2O3/GQD3 nanoparticles.
SEM images (a, b) and XRD patterns (c) of Gd2O3-1 and Gd2O3-2.
Relaxation rate of 1/T 1 (a), 1/T 2 (b) and T 1-weighted (c), T 2-weighted (d) MR image of composite nanoparticles at different concentrations of Gd ion.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Foundation item: Project supported by Guizhou Provincial Education Department Project ([2017]016) and Guizhou Science and Technology Project ([2019]2165).

head.ce:title: Preparation and properties of bifunctional Gd2O3/GQD composite nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Metallurgy, University of Guizhou, Guiyang 550025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Metallurgy, University of Guizhou, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Local Co-construction Engineering Laboratory for High Performance Metal Structure Material and Manufacture Technology, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Local Co-construction Engineering Laboratory for High Performance Metal Structure Material and Manufacture Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Local Co-construction Engineering Laboratory for High Performance Metal Structure Material and Manufacture Technology, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Guiyang University, Guiyang 550003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Guiyang University, Guiyang 550003, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials and Metallurgy, University of Guizhou, Guiyang 550025 China. Tel.: +86-851-83627683.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanocomposites of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified gadolinium oxide (Gd2O3) particles were successfully synthesized. In addition, the effects of the amount of GQDs and the particle size of Gd2O3 on the properties of nanocomposites were investigated. The structures and properties of nanoparticles were characterized by X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, photoluminescence spectrometry, and magnetic resonance imaging. The results show that the synthesized particles Gd2O3/GQD are spherical with a diameter of approximately 40 nm and have excellent fluorescence and magnetic property, and its relaxation rate (r 1 = 25.18 (mmol/L)−1/s) is higher than that of the typical commercial contrast agents Gd-DTPA (r 1 = 4.5 (mmol/L)−1/s). The fluorescence property of Gd2O3/GQD is optimized by varying the V GQDs:V Gd 2 O 3 ratio, and 1:1 ratio of V GQDs:V Gd 2 O 3 results in the best fluorescence property. The large particles of Gd2O3 improve the relaxation property of nanocomposites. Therefore, the nanocomposites Gd2O3/GQD with magnetic and fluorescence characteristics can be used in biological imaging, drug targeting, and in-vivo diagnostic analysis.

Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles are composed of GQDs wrapped in Gd2O3 particles. The spin magnetic moment of Gd3+ in Gd2O3 and the dipole effect of 1H nuclear magnetic moment can shorten the longitudinal relaxation time of hydrogen protons and improve MRI contrast. The excellent biological compatibility and fluorescence properties of GQDs make the surface of Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles emit blue-green light. The groups on the surface are conducive to binding with small biological molecules to realize the application of drug carriers and targeted drug delivery.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, magnetic fluorescent nanomaterials with core–shell structure have attracted much attention and been widely used in multimodality imaging, medical targeting, diagnosis, and treatment. 1–3 Owing to the characteristic properties of both core and shell, the suitable shell selection is beneficial to obtaining magnetic fluorescent nanomaterials with low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility. 4–6

The paramagnetic composites based on Gd are widely used as positive contrast agents for clinical magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), 7–10 owing to their high spin magnetic moment of Gd (III) with seven unpaired 4f electrons. 11
,
12 The Gd3+ ions in Gd2O3 particles coordinately induce the longitudinal relaxation of water protons and result in a high T 1 value compared to Gd chelates. 13
,
14 However, inorganic Gd2O3 nanoparticles are toxic, hydrophobic, and have poor biocompatibility. Hence, encapsulating them with hydrophilic ligands to improve the possibility of medical applications is needed. In our previous study, 15
,
16 lanthanide ions (Tb3+, Eu3+) were doped into Gd2O3 by a polyalcohol way (diethylene glycol, DEG). Fe3O4@Gd2O3:Tb3+@SiO X (polysiloxane) nanoparticles (r 1 = 6.4 (mmol/L)−1/s) were synthesized by heterogeneous nucleation, and their relaxation property was better than that of the commercial Gd-DTPA (r 1 = 4.5 (mmol/L)−1/s). 17
,
18 Gd2O3 nanoparticles were encapsulated by a polysiloxane shell to form the core–shell particles, and the functional groups in polysiloxane could be combined with other biomolecules to realize biological detection of core–shell nanoparticles.

As a branch of the graphene family, GQDs are non-toxic and zero-dimensional fluorescence nanomaterial. Owing to their unique photoluminescence and biocompatibility, they have been widely used in biological imaging. 19–23 The edge of GQDs has a variety of reactive groups (such as carboxylates and amines), which are useful for further modification with other functional molecules. 24
,
25 GQDs with modified coating can introduce fluorescence property into magnetic particles, which will be beneficial to developing dual-function nanomaterials with magnetism and fluorescence.

GQDs can be considered as a good candidate of fluorescent nanoparticles to be combined with magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) to achieve bifunctional properties. MNPs are usually combined with the edges of GQDs through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions. Based on easily modified characteristics of GQDs, Fe3O4/GQDs@GM (gelatin) was prepared by a co-precipitation method and had good imaging property and could be used as a drug carrier to biodegrade cancer cells in the vicinity of tumor tissue. 26 GQDs-MnO2 fluorescence sensor has been obtained by π-π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, promising that glutathione (GSH) fluorescence sensor has been reported for the analysis of complex systems and quality control in the pharmaceutical industry. 27 Similarly, NiCo-GQDs-graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized as a newly developed carrier for hydrophobic anticancer drug delivery. 28 Moreover, GQDs@Fe3O4/molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) nanocomposites were obtained by a sol–gel polymerization reaction to be used as fluorescent probes and optical sensors. 29 Recently, Wang et al. 30 synthesized a magnetic and fluorescent Gd2O3/GQD nanocomposite with good biocompatibility, and the longitudinal relaxation rate r 1 of the obtained nanocomposite reached 15.99 (mmol/L)−1/s, indicating that it can be used as an ideal non-toxic dual-modal imaging agent. However, the controllable preparation of this nanocomposite and the enhancement of the properties need to be further studied.

In this study, Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles were prepared by combining GQDs with Gd2O3 nanoparticles, and the factors (e.g., volume ratio of GQDs and Gd2O3, particle size) affecting the fluorescent and magnetic properties of Gd2O3/GQD were further studied. The small size of GQDs has better water permeability and can improve the MRI performance of composite materials. Multifunctional composite materials can provide clearer imaging and optimize the drug delivery system, which is conducive to combining a variety of detection techniques to provide more personalized solutions for clinical treatment. This study presents a feasible way for the controllable synthesis and property improvement of Gd2O3/GQD, and the experimental results provide a theoretical basis for the biological application of the bifunctional nanocomposites.

The materials used in this study were as follows: citric acid (AR, 99.5%), sodium hydroxide (AR ≥96.0%), deionized water, B-wide pH test paper, tetrahydrofuran (THF, 99.5%), gadolinium chloride hexahydrate (GdCl3⋅6H2O, 99.9%), diethylene glycol (DEG, 99%), polyethylene glycol (PEG, 99%).

GQDs were prepared by a pyrolysis method: Citric acid was heated in an oil bath (30 min, 200 °C) until the citric acid solid turned into orange-red colloid, followed by adding 10 mol/L NaOH solution. The solution was stirred for 10 min until fully mixed, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The as-obtained pristine GQDs alkaline solution was stored in cold place, to avoid the exposure of light.

Gd2O3 nanoparticles were prepared by diethylene glycol method 31 : GdCl3 ‧6H2O was added into 200 mL DEG at room temperature, and then stirred for 12 h until the solid was completely dissolved. NaOH (10 mol/L) was dissolved in 200 mL DEG and stirred for 6 h. The solution was dried for 160 h (120 °C) in an air blast drying chamber, and the resulting Gd2O3 particles were calcined in a heat treatment furnace at 800 °C for 2 h to improve the crystallinity of particles.

The appropriate amount of Gd2O3 particles were dispersed in THF and stirred for 1 h. The Gd2O3-THF solution (5 mg/mL) was added in 10 mL pristine GQDs solution, and the mixture was stirred for 0.5 h to make a clear reaction, followed by heating at 70 °C for 0.5 h to completely evaporate THF. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution was centrifuged to remove the unreacted Gd2O3 nanoparticles and further dialyzed through a 100 kda dialysis bag for 3 d, and the Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles were finally obtained (see Fig. 1 ).

In order to study the effect of the amount of GQDs on the fluorescence property of Gd2O3/GQD, V GQDs:V Gd 2 O 3 with the ratios of 1:2, 1:1, and 3:2 was used to synthesize Gd2O3/GQD, and the resulting materials were labeled as Gd2O3/GQD1, Gd2O3/GQD2, and Gd2O3/GQD3, respectively. In order to investigate the effect of the as-synthesized Gd2O3 using different solvents on the relaxation property, the Gd2O3 nanoparticles were prepared by solvents DEG (Gd2O3-1) and PEG (Gd2O3-2), and the resulting Gd2O3-1 and Gd2O3-2 were reacted with equal volume of GQDs, which were labeled as Gd2O3-1/GQD2 and Gd2O3-2/GQD2, respectively.

The morphologies of nanocomposites were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai F20, FEI, USA) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SUPRA 40, Zeiss, Germany). The crystal structure was examined by X-ray diffractometry (XRD, X'Pert Powder, Panalytical, Netherlands) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm) at a scanning rate of 5 (°)/min over 2θ range from 20° to 70°. Infrared spectra were recorded by Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher, USA), with a resolution of 4 cm−1 in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 by the KBr pellet technique. X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded using an Axis Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrophotometer (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher, USA) equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). Photoluminescence emission spectra were obtained in the range of 300–650 nm, with a slit width of 5 nm, using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent, USA) with a 150 W xenon lamp and a resolution of 4 nm. The weighted images and relaxation rates of nanoparticles were measured using a 4.7 T magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scanner (Biospec 4.7/30, Bruker, Germany).

Fig. 2 shows the TEM and SAED images of Gd2O3/GQD. Fig. 2(a, b) show the uniform distribution of spherical Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles in the size range of 30–40 nm, and the light gray GQDs nanoparticles wrap around the black Gd2O3 nanoparticles. Fig. 2(c) shows the lattice fringes of 0.19 nm corresponding to the (440) crystal planes of cubic phase Gd2O3, and the lattice fringes of 0.24 nm correspond to the (100) crystal plane of a single graphite. 32 The cubic phase Gd2O3 in Gd2O3/GQD is proved by the SAED model in Fig. 2(d) and is consistent with the corresponding crystal planes of Gd2O3. 33

The elemental compositions of GQDs and Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles are verified by XPS in Fig. 3 (a–c). Fig. 3(a) shows the full scan XPS spectra of GQDs and Gd2O3/GQD with two most obvious peaks of C 1s signal at 285 eV and the O 1s signal at 531 eV, respectively. The full scan XPS spectra of Gd2O3/GQD exhibit peaks corresponding to C 1s and O 1s, and the full spectrum of Gd at 150 eV is also confirmed. The C1s spectra of GQDs and Gd2O3/GQD in Fig. 3(b) show three distinct peaks at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to the C–C, C–O, and CO of graphene. 34 The C 1s spectra of Gd2O3/GQD present all the characteristic peaks of C 1s spectra of GQDs, confirming the presence of GQDs in Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles. Fig. 3(c) shows the Gd 4 d spectra of Gd2O3/GQD with obvious peaks at 148.1 and 141.9 eV, corresponding to Gd 4d3/2 and Gd 4d5/2 levels, respectively. 35 The infrared spectra of the nanoparticles (Fig. 3(d)) show the absorption peak at 3436 cm−1 attributed to the O–H bond caused by the surface water absorption of Gd2O3 nanoparticles, and the absorption peak at 544 cm−1 corresponds to the Gd–O bond of Gd2O3. 36 The apparent absorption peak at 3430 cm−1 is attributed to the surface hydroxyl group of GQDs, 37 and the peaks at 1601, 1405, and 1090 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of CC, 38 the bending vibration of C–H, 39 and the stretching vibration of C–OH, 40 respectively. In summary, the presence of all the characteristic peaks of Gd2O3 and GQDs indicates successful formation of composite Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of amount of GQDs on fluorescence property of Gd2O3/GQD
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4 displays the TEM and AFM images of the as-prepared GQDs. Fig. 4(a) shows that the GQDs are uniformly distributed particles with a particle size in the range of 4–6 nm. The distance between the lattice fringes of GQDs (insets in Fig. 4(a)) is approximately 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (1100) crystal plane of graphene. 41
,
42 The AFM image (Fig. 4(b)) shows uniform distribution of GQDs nanoparticles, with particle height approximately in the range of 0.8–1.4 nm and an average height of 1.2 nm.

Fig. 5 (a–c) show the SEM images of Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles prepared with different amounts of GQDs. Fig. 5(a) presents a relatively low number of nanoparticles Gd2O3/GQD1 in the case of V GQDs:V Gd 2 O 3 = 1:2, and the size of the particles with poor dispersion is approximately 30 nm and not uniform. Because of insufficient quantity of GQDs, a few unreacted Gd2O3 particles remain, and the size of the composite nanoparticles is small. When V GQDs:V Gd 2 O 3 = 1:1 (Fig. 5(b)), the Gd2O3/GQD2 nanoparticles with better dispersion are obtained with the size of approximately 40 nm and a less number of small particles. Almost all hydrophobic Gd2O3 nanoparticles react with GQDs. When V GQDs:V Gd 2 O 3 = 3:2 (Fig. 5(c)), the size of the nanocomposites Gd2O3/GQD3 hardly changes. However, the number of nanoparticles decreases and particles begin to aggregate. Fig. 5(d)–(f) show the emission spectra of Gd2O3/GQD1, Gd2O3/GQD2, and Gd2O3/GQD3. When the excitation wavelength increases from 350 to 420 nm, the emission wavelength is nearly excitation independent, and the fluorescence intensity of Gd2O3/GQD1 and Gd2O3/GQD3 increases gradually (Fig. 5(d, f)). With the change of the excitation wavelength from 420 to 440 nm, the fluorescence spectra of Gd2O3/GQD1 and Gd2O3/GQD3 exhibit a blue shift, accompanied by a decrease in the fluorescence intensity. This phenomenon can be explained by the different sizes or emission sites of each sp2 clusters in graphite. 43
Fig. 5(g) displays the fluorescence property of GQDs and Gd2O3/GQD prepared with different amounts of GQDs. The fluorescence intensity of Gd2O3/GQD solution is slightly lower than that of GQDs. In the case of V GQDs:V Gd 2 O 3 = 1:1, the fluorescence intensity of Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles is the highest, owing to the sufficient reaction of Gd2O3 with GQDs. The increased yield of the composite particles enhances the fluorescence intensity. When V GQDs:V Gd 2 O 3 = 3:2, because of the excessive amount of GQDs, the concentration of GQDs in the solution is relatively high, resulting in fluorescence quenching of Gd2O3/GQD. 44

Based on the fluorescence emission spectra of the nanoparticles (Fig. 5(g)), the CIE chromaticity coordinates are established, as shown in Fig. 6 . All the coordinates of emission intensities are located in the blue-green region. The Gd2O3/GQD1, Gd2O3/GQD2, Gd2O3/GQD3 nanoparticles have coordinates of (0.1519, 0.1698) (0.1571, 0.1878), and (0.1571, 0.1825), respectively. Among them, Gd2O3/GQD2 has the best color saturation and the best fluorescence property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solvent on relaxation property of Gd2O3/GQD
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 7 (a, b) show the SEM images of Gd2O3 synthesized in different solvents. The Gd2O3-1 (in DEG) and Gd2O3-2 (in PEG) particles both exhibit spherical shape with entirely different diameters in the ranges of 20–30 nm and 70–100 nm, respectively. As the chains of PEG will intertwine with each other and facilitate the aggregation of particles, the size of the nanoparticles will increase. 45
Fig. 7(c) shows the XRD patterns of Gd2O3-1 and Gd2O3-2, and all the diffraction peaks correspond to the cubic Gd2O3 crystal lattice (JCPDS No. 86–2477). 46 The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28.6°, 33.2° 47.6°, and 56.5° correspond to the (222) (400) (440), and (622) crystal planes of the cubic Gd2O3 crystal, respectively.

Fig. 8 shows the magnetic properties of Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles prepared using different solvents. The longitudinal relaxation rates r 1 (1/T 1) of Gd2O3-2/GQD2 and Gd2O3-1/GQD2 in Fig. 8(a) are 25.18 and 22.09 (mmol/L)−1/s, respectively. With increasing equivalent Gd3+ concentration, the brightness of the T 1-weighted image increases gradually (Fig. 8(c)). The quantitative signal intensity of Gd2O3-2/GQD2 is slightly stronger than that of Gd2O3-1/GQD2. Because of the decrease in the effective total surface area or S/V (surface area/volume) ratio of large-sized Gd2O3 particles (Gd2O3-2/GQD2), the space for water molecules to diffuse on the surface of the large-sized nanocomposites is large, which shortens the longitudinal relaxation time of water protons. Fig. 8(b) shows that the relaxation rates r 2 (1/T 2) of Gd2O3-2/GQD2 and Gd2O3-1/GQD2 are 35.01 and 44.14 (mmol/L)−1/s, respectively. Fig. 8(d) shows that the concentration dependent T 2-weighted images darken with increasing Gd3+ concentration. The relaxation rate ratios r 2/r 1 of Gd2O3-2/GQD2 and Gd2O3-1/GQD2 are 1.39 and 2, respectively, which are suitable for T 1 MRI contrast agent (r 2/r 1 < 10). Compared to commercial contrast agents (Gd-DTPA, r 1 = 4.5 (mmol/L)−1/s), the longitudinal relaxation rate r 1 of Gd2O3/GQD nanocomposites is significantly improved.

In conclusion, dual-functional Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles with core–shell structure were successfully prepared and exhibit PL/MRI imaging capabilities. Gd2O3 cores are easy for magnetic manipulation and magnetic imaging, and external water-soluble GQDs provide strong blue-green fluorescence. In the case of V GQDs:V Gd 2 O 3 = 1:1, the composite Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles exhibit the optimal fluorescence property. The large particles of Gd2O3 prepared using PEG as a solvent improve the relaxation property, and the relaxation rate of the resulting Gd2O3/GQD is 5–6 times higher than of the commercial Gd chelate. The dual-functional Gd2O3/GQD nanoparticles can be used for fluorescence imaging and MRI detection.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple approach for the synthesis of bifunctional Fe3O4@Gd2O3:Eu3+ core–shell nanocomposites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Peng HX, Cui B, Li LL, Wang YS. A simple approach for the synthesis of bifunctional Fe3O4@Gd2O3:Eu3+ core-shell nanocomposites. J. Alloys Compd. 2012; 531:30.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green one-pot synthesis of carboxymethylcellulose/Zn-based metal-organic framework/graphene oxide bio-nanocomposite as a nanocarrier for drug delivery system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Javanbakht, S, Pooresmaeil M, Namazi H. Green one-pot synthesis of carboxymethylcellulose/Zn-based metal-organic framework/graphene oxide bio-nanocomposite as a nanocarrier for drug delivery system. Carbohydr. Polym. 2019; 208: 294.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Paramagnetic properties of metal-free boron-doped graphene quantum dots and their application for safe magnetic resonance imaging

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bifunctional Fe3O4@Gd2O3:Eu3+ nanocomposites obtained by the homogeneous precipitation method

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Peng HX, Cui B, Wang YS. Bifunctional Fe3O4@Gd2O3:Eu3+ nanocomposites obtained by the homogeneous precipitation method. Mater. Res. Bull. 2013; 48(5): 1767.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasmall Fe@Fe3O4 nanoparticles as T 1-T 2 dual-mode MRI contrast agents for targeted tumor imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu DL, Li JJ, Wang CB, An L, Lin JM, Tian QW, et al. Ultrasmall Fe@Fe3O4 nanoparticles as T1-T2 dual-mode MRI contrast agents for targeted tumor imaging. Nanomed. Nanotechnol. 2020; 32: 102335.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of mesoporous core-shell structured GdPO4:Eu@SiO2@mSiO2 nanorods for drug delivery and cell imaging applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wu YL, Xu XZ, You XL, Xiao Q. Synthesis of mesoporous core-shell structured GdPO4:Eu@SiO2@mSiO2 nanorods for drug delivery and cell imaging applications. J. Rare Earths. 2020; 38(10): 1086.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybrid gadolinium oxide nanoparticles: multimodal contrast agents for in vivo imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bridot JL, Faure AC, Laurent S, Riviere C, Billotey C, Hiba B, et al. Hybrid gadolinium oxide nanoparticles: multimodal contrast agents for in vivo imaging. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2007; 129(16): 5076.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrafine PEG-capped gadolinia nanoparticles: cytotoxicity and potential biomedical applications for MRI and luminescent imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Atabaev TS, Lee JH, Han DW, Kim HK, Hwang YH. Ultrafine PEG-capped gadolinia nanoparticles: cytotoxicity and potential biomedical applications for MRI and luminescent imaging. RSC Adv. 2014; 4(65): 34343.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of paramagnetic graphene quantum dots as a platform for simultaneous dual-modality bioimaging and tumor-targeted drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Huang CL, Huang CC, Mai FD, Yen CL, Tzing SH, Hsieh HT, et al. Application of paramagnetic graphene quantum dots as a platform for simultaneous dual-modality bioimaging and tumor-targeted drug delivery. J. Mater. Chem. B. 2015; 3(4): 651.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-resolution MRI analysis of breast cancer xenograft on the chick chorioallantoic membrane

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zuo Z, Syrovets T, Genze F, Abaei A, Ma G, Simmet T, et al. High-resolution MRI analysis of breast cancer xenograft on the chick chorioallantoic membrane. NMR Biomed. 2015; 28(4): 440.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles: synthesis, stabilization, vectorization, physicochemical characterizations, and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Laurent S, Forge D, Port M, Roch A, Robic C, Vander Elst L, et al. Magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles: synthesis, stabilization, vectorization, physicochemical characterizations, and biological applications. Chem. Rev. 2008; 108(6): 2064.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gadolinium agents for theragnosis of malignant tumors

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Paramagnetic ultrasmall gadolinium oxide nanoparticles as advanced T1 MRI contrast agent: account for largelongitudinal relaxivity, optimal particle diameter, and in vivo T1 MR images
